cards.text: To use file streams, you must {{c1::#include <fstream>}}.
The three file stream classes are {{c1::std::ifstream}} (reading), {{c2::std::ofstream}} (writing), and {{c3::std::fstream}} (both reading and writing).
To open a file for reading, use {{c1::std::ifstream file("data.txt")}} or {{c2::file.open("data.txt")}}.

cards.front: How do you check if a file was successfully opened?
cards.back: Use if (file.is_open()) or if (file). The stream evaluates to false if the file couldn't be opened.

cards.front: What is the difference between std::ios::out and std::ios::app?
cards.back: std::ios::out overwrites the file (truncates it). std::ios::app appends to the end of the file without erasing existing content.

cards.question: How do you read an entire file line by line?
cards.explanation: getline() returns the stream, which evaluates to false when EOF is reached or an error occurs.

cards.front: Do you need to manually close file streams?
cards.back: No. File streams automatically close when they go out of scope (RAII). However, you can manually close with file.close() if needed.

cards.text: To open a file in binary mode, combine with {{c1::std::ios::binary}}, like: std::ios::in | std::ios::binary

cards.concept: Proper file I/O error handling pattern
cards.explanation:
Always check if file operations succeeded:
  • Check if file opened: if (!file) return error;
  • Check if reads succeeded: if (!(file >> data))
  • Use is_open() for explicit open checks
  • Check eof() vs fail() to distinguish end-of-file from errors

cards.question: How do you write data to a file?
cards.explanation: Use ofstream with the << operator, just like cout. The file is automatically created if it doesn't exist.

cards.front: What happens if you open a file with std::ofstream and the file already exists?
cards.back: By default, ofstream truncates (erases) the existing file content. Use std::ios::app to append instead.
